summary: The paper has undergone significant improvement since the pre-R4 critic pass: H3 (cross-domain) is now honestly reported as FALSIFIED with a proper shuffled control, and the paper pivots to a two-contribution structure (EDA regime-specific detector + three-subtype taxonomy). Honest negative-result reporting is a genuine strength. However, six critical and six major problems remain. The most severe: (1) the three-subtype taxonomy's central claim ('early absorption dominates at 72-75%') is acutely threshold-sensitive — at tau=0.2 the split is 32%/34%/34%, reversing the dominance claim; (2) EDA is mathematically identical to the existing SAEBench 'encoder_decoder_cosine_sim' metric (Pearson r=0.9999), making novelty claims hinge entirely on a formal theorem that is stated without a valid proof; (3) the Theorem 1 proof sketch is circular; (4) EDA fails on 5/8 SAE configurations including L12-65k (the configuration with the most absorbed latents); (5) the R4 direct-label EDA validation used GPT-2 only (Gemma 2B and Llama-3.1-8B remain gated), so the paper's primary model (Gemma 2B Scope) still has no exact-label AUROC validation; (6) the abstract's +0.396 AUROC baseline comparison figure belongs to L5-16k but is stated adjacent to the L12-16k AUROC=0.776, creating a misleading juxtaposition. The paper in its current state is viable for a workshop or mid-tier venue (EMNLP 2026 / NeurIPS MI Workshop) but requires at minimum six targeted fixes before any submission.

findings.description: The 'early absorption dominance' finding (72-75% early-type), the paper's central actionable claim, is acutely sensitive to the cosine threshold tau. The r4_writing_gate.json explicitly records: 'at tau=0.2 early proportion drops to ~32% at L12-65k — dominance is threshold-dependent.' At tau=0.2 the split is approximately 32%/34%/34% (early/late/partial) — no category dominates. At tau=0.3 the split becomes 72%/14%/14%. At tau=0.4 it becomes 95%/3%/2%. The paper reports threshold stability of the late > early EDA ordering across tau=0.2-0.4 (which is true) but obscures that the prevalence proportions — and thus the 'early dominance' claim — vary by a factor of 2-3x across this same range. The current paper does not adequately disclose the magnitude of this sensitivity in the main text.
findings.suggestion: Add a table in Section 6.2 showing early/late/partial prevalence at tau = {0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.35, 0.4} for both L12-16k and L12-65k. Qualify the 'early dominance' claim as 'at the canonical tau=0.3 threshold, conditional on a scientific justification for why tau=0.3 is the appropriate boundary.' Provide that justification (e.g., 95th percentile of random pairwise cosine similarities, or calibrated against SynthSAEBench labels). If the justification is insufficient, downgrade 'dominance' to 'plurality at tau=0.3' in the abstract and conclusions.

findings.description: EDA (1 - cos(w_{e,j}, d_j)) is mathematically identical to 1 - encoder_decoder_cosine_sim, which SAEBench already computes for every SAE configuration. The paper reports Pearson r=0.9999 between EDA and the SAEBench metric (phase1_eda_deda_validation.json confirms correlations of 0.9997-0.9999 across all configs). The R4 validation (r4a_eda_direct_validation.json) confirms EDA = 1 - dec_cos identity: 'EDA and decoder_cosine AUROC identical (delta=0.0 on both GPT-2 configs).' The r4_writing_gate.json explicitly lists as a limitation: 'EDA = 1 - dec_cosine: not independently novel as a formula (exists in SAEBench); novelty is in systematic regime characterization.' The paper's Introduction still states 'We introduce EDA, a weight-only absorption screening metric' without disclosing the SAEBench precedent. A reviewer familiar with SAEBench (likely for a top-tier venue) will flag this as a fatal novelty problem.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit paragraph in Section 3.1 before the metric definition: 'The cosine similarity between encoder row j and decoder column j is already reported in SAEBench (Karvonen et al., 2025) under the label encoder_decoder_cosine_sim. EDA formalizes this observation by (1) deriving a formal lower bound connecting encoder-decoder misalignment to absorption degree (Theorem 1), (2) systematically evaluating the quantity as an absorption classifier against ground-truth labels across multiple SAE configurations with AUROC measurement, and (3) characterizing its operating regime across layers and widths. EDA does not introduce a novel formula; its contribution is systematic empirical validation and theoretical grounding of an existing structural observation.' This preempts the rejection while honestly repositioning the contribution.

findings.description: Theorem 1 (the EDA Lower Bound) is stated as a formal theorem with a proof sketch that is circular. The proof sketch states that 'absorption introduces a perturbation: by the delta-absorption definition, the gradient contribution from child-active inputs pushes w_{e,j} away from d_j by an amount proportional to delta * ||d_c|| * sin(theta_{jc}).' This assertion IS the key claim that needs to be proven, not assumed. The quantity delta is defined inline as 'magnitude of suppression of latent j due to child c' — this is not a mathematical definition but a verbal description. No formal link from the SDL stationarity condition to the perturbation bound delta * sin(theta) is provided. A reviewer with optimization theory background (expected for NeurIPS / ICLR) will reject Theorem 1 as stated. Moreover, Appendix B referenced twice in the main text (Sections 3.3 and 7.1) is absent from paper.md.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) provide a genuine proof in Appendix B: formally define delta-absorption as the magnitude of the bias term in the encoder's first-order stationarity condition induced by child-active inputs, derive the perturbation bound from the SDL loss geometry at a partial minimum, and show the algebraic steps from residual magnitude to the cosine-distance bound; or (b) downgrade Theorem 1 to 'Proposition 1 (Intuition)' and add: 'We provide a heuristic argument; a formal proof requires specifying the full biconvex landscape geometry at partial minima, which we leave to future work. The bound is empirically consistent with SynthSAEBench results in Section 3.4.' Write Appendix B with the conditioning analysis for D-EDA even if the theorem is downgraded.

findings.description: EDA fails on 5/8 SAE configurations including the configuration most important for the taxonomy (L12-65k, where n=65 absorbed latents provides the most statistical power): AUROC=0.468 (below chance), Cohen's d=-0.152 (reversed). The configuration with 4x more absorbed latents than any other shows EDA performing worse than random. This is not adequately resolved by the R4 experiments: the R4 direct-label validation (r4a_eda_direct_validation.json) applied Chanin et al. labels only to GPT-2 SAEs (which already had direct labels in R3). Gemma 2B and Llama-3.1-8B remain gated. The paper's primary claim ('EDA achieves AUROC=0.776 on Gemma Scope L12-16k') still uses Neuronpedia proxy labels. It is possible that the L12-16k AUROC=0.776 with proxy labels is equally inflated as the L12-65k pilot (AUROC=0.853 pilot vs 0.468 full).
findings.suggestion: The abstract should explicitly note that Gemma results use proxy labels and GPT-2 provides the only exact-label validation. Add: 'Gemma Scope results use Neuronpedia proxy labels because Gemma 2 2B remains gated on HuggingFace at time of writing; the only exact-label validation is GPT-2 Small L6 (AUROC=0.629, n_pos=67).' The L12-16k result should be presented with appropriate uncertainty about proxy label reliability. Report whether the L12-16k pilot-to-full discrepancy was audited.

findings.description: The three-subtype taxonomy's statistical foundation is weak at L12-16k (n=16 absorbed latents). At L12-16k, KW p=0.237 and MW p=0.220 — both NON-SIGNIFICANT at all 5 tested thresholds. The EDA ordering prediction (late > partial > early) fails at L12-16k: eda_ordering_holds=false for all thresholds. The paper claims the taxonomy is 'confirmed' and cites 'threshold stability' — but stability of a non-significant, ordering-failing result across thresholds does not constitute confirmation. The paper's abstract and Section 6.2 give the impression both configurations support the taxonomy when only L12-65k does statistically.
findings.suggestion: Restate the taxonomy evidence level clearly: 'The subtype EDA ordering (late > early) is statistically confirmed at L12-65k (n=65, KW p=0.0002) but not at L12-16k (n=16, KW p=0.237). The L12-16k result is underpowered. Expanding the taxonomy analysis to additional SAE configurations with sufficient absorbed latents (n >= 50) is required for the cross-configuration claim.' Run taxonomy on GPT-2-L6 (n_pos=67 exact labels, most reliable configuration) as a third data point.

findings.description: The EDA ordering prediction for the partial subtype fails in both configurations. At L12-65k, the predicted ordering is late > partial > early. The observed ordering has partial median EDA LOWER than early median EDA (partial median=0.652, early median=0.668 at L12-65k threshold 0.3). The eda_ordering_holds field is false in phase2a_taxonomy.json for both configurations at all thresholds tested. The paper's abstract and body state 'EDA ordering (late > partial > early) holds at L12-65k (KW p=0.0002)' — but the KW test rejects the null of equal group means, NOT the null of the specific ordering late > partial > early. The specific three-way ordering prediction is violated.
findings.suggestion: Correct the claim in the abstract and Section 6.2: 'The ordering late > early holds at all 5 tested thresholds for both configurations (threshold-stable). The predicted full ordering late > partial > early is violated: partial-type latents have lower median EDA than early-type latents at both configurations and all thresholds, suggesting that the decoder-coverage threshold (tau) partitions latents in a way that does not cleanly correspond to EDA ranking. The partial/early distinction may share geometric EDA properties while differing in decoder dictionary coverage.' This is honest and actually adds mechanistic insight.

findings.description: The abstract contains a misleading DeLong comparison: 'EDA achieves AUROC = 0.776 on Gemma Scope L12-16k... outperforming the decoder cosine baseline by +0.396 AUROC.' The +0.396 figure is from L5-16k (EDA=0.698 vs decoder cosine=0.302), NOT from L12-16k. At L12-16k, the comparison is EDA=0.776 vs decoder cosine=0.224, a difference of +0.553. The abstract presents these figures in a single sentence making it appear 0.396 is the L12-16k advantage. The paper's DeLong test in Section 4.2 correctly reports 'at L12-16k: difference = +0.553' but the abstract is ambiguous.
findings.suggestion: Revise the abstract to: 'EDA achieves AUROC = 0.776 on Gemma Scope L12-16k [0.700, 0.863], outperforming the decoder cosine baseline by +0.553 AUROC at L12-16k (DeLong p ≈ 0), and AUROC = 0.629 on GPT-2 Small L6 [0.561, 0.692] with exact Chanin et al. labels.' This correctly attributes the comparison to L12-16k and introduces the GPT-2 result as the exact-label anchor.

findings.description: The ITAC null test on early-type latents does not apply ITAC to early-type latents. The paper claims '0% FN reduction on early/partial-type latents, validating subtype selectivity' (Table 4 comment 'Confirms 0% FN reduction; validates subtype selectivity: ITAC operates exactly where the taxonomy predicts'). But examining phase2b_itac.json: the null_test_early field reports the baseline FN rates of early-type latents WITHOUT applying ITAC. ITAC was never applied to early-type latents. A 0% FN reduction when you do not apply the correction is not evidence of selectivity — it is a tautology.
findings.suggestion: Apply the ITAC correction procedure to early-type latents: attempt D-EDA decomposition and parent candidate identification for each early-type latent. Report what fraction of early-type latents have identifiable parent candidates (expected: 0, since by definition early-type means no decoder direction near parent probe). If 0/n_early have candidates, report this as 'ITAC cannot identify correction targets for early-type latents by construction (max cos(d_k, v_p) < tau for all decoder columns), confirming 0 applicable targets' — this is the actual null test. If any candidates are found, apply ITAC and verify FN change.

findings.description: The SynthSAEBench validation (AUROC=1.000, F1=0.974) is tautological. Absorption is injected into synthetic SAEs by rotating encoder directions away from decoder directions toward child decoder directions. EDA measures encoder-decoder misalignment. Perfect discrimination on synthetic data where absorption IS defined as encoder-decoder misalignment does not validate EDA's ability to detect behavioral absorption (Chanin et al.'s definition: a parent latent fails to fire when a child is active). The SynthSAEBench validation confirms EDA correctly computes cosine misalignment, nothing more.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit disclaimer in Section 3.4: 'Note that SynthSAEBench injects absorption precisely as encoder-decoder rotation — the same geometric mechanism EDA is designed to detect. AUROC=1.000 on synthetic data therefore confirms EDA's computation is correct, not that it detects behavioral absorption (as defined by Chanin et al.) in trained SAEs. The latter validation is performed in Section 4 against real SAE labels.' This is honest and preempts the tautology objection.

findings.description: The polysemanticity stratification reports AUROC=0.922 [0.842, 0.979] on polysemantic latents at L12-16k. The ablation_polysemanticity.json shows n_pos=3 positive examples in the polysemantic stratum. Bootstrap confidence intervals with n_pos=3 are unreliable regardless of bootstrap sample size (1000 or 10000): three positive examples produce only 3 distinct AUROC values under leave-one-out, and the CI [0.842, 0.979] likely reflects numerical artifacts of the bootstrap. The main text reports this CI without caveat.
findings.suggestion: Add a footnote to Section 4.4: 'The polysemantic AUROC CI [0.842, 0.979] derives from n_pos=3 positive examples; bootstrap CIs with n_pos < 10 are unreliable and should be interpreted as illustrative only.' Consider removing the polysemantic AUROC from the main text or moving to an appendix, retaining only the monosemantic result (n_pos=13, more defensible).

findings.description: The cross-domain section (Section 5) framing has a consistency gap between the shuffled control (run on n=3 SAE configs, R4B pilot) and the intra-RAVEL coherence (computed from the full analysis on n=6 SAE configs, R3 phase3e). The paper presents intra-RAVEL rho=0.924 as 'suggestive existence evidence' in Section 5.3, but a careful reviewer will note: the shuffled control that invalidated the absolute rates was run on only half the configs. The R4 shuffled control with n=3 configs reports intra-RAVEL rho=0.667 (r4_writing_gate.json: 'R4 pilot, n=3 SAE configs'). The R3 rho=0.924 was computed on n=6 configs with invalid Qwen2.5-0.5B probes. The paper needs to disclose this experiment-size discrepancy.
findings.suggestion: Add to Section 5.3: 'The intra-RAVEL coherence rho=0.924 derives from the full R3 cross-domain experiment (n=6 SAE configurations, phase3e_crossdomain_analysis.json), while the shuffled hierarchy control was run on a pilot subset of 3 SAE configurations. The pilot-level rho for the 3-config subset is 0.667. The coherence finding is therefore not directly challenged by the shuffled control (which tests absolute rates, not relative rankings), but it is also unvalidated by it. A same-model probe replication is needed to establish whether the rho=0.924 coherence reflects genuine absorption structure or probe-quality artifacts.'

findings.description: The Section 7.1 practical recommendation claims: 'focusing on the top 5% of latents by EDA contains approximately 56% of absorbed latents (Prec@50 = 0.0035), reducing the supervised evaluation budget by 20x.' These numbers are not derivable from the data. At L12-16k: top 5% of 16,384 latents = 819 candidates; 819 * Prec@50 (0.0035) ≈ 2.87 absorbed; 2.87 / 16 total positives ≈ 18%, not 56%. The '20x' reduction is unexplained. The writing quality review also flagged this (review.md Issue 5). No source data supports the 56% and 20x figures.
findings.suggestion: Either derive these figures correctly from the source data and show the calculation, or replace with the directly supportable claim: 'At L12-16k, Prec@50 = 0.0035 — absorbed latents are 3.5x enriched in the top-50 EDA candidates relative to random. Using EDA to prioritize 5% of the dictionary (819 latents in a 16k SAE) for supervised inspection provides a meaningful but modest enrichment, reducing evaluation effort while maintaining partial coverage.'

findings.description: Three figures (5, 6, 7) are referenced in the main text but their PDF generation status is uncertain. The writing/review.md confirms 'All seven figure PDFs are present in the writing/figures/ directory' but the visual audit casts doubt on whether Fig5 and Fig6 captions are aligned with the current null-result framing for cross-domain. Additionally, Appendix B (referenced twice: Sections 3.3, 7.1) and Appendix C (RAVEL probe details) are absent from paper.md. The SynthSAEBench citation 'synthasbench2026' is flagged as potentially a placeholder.
findings.suggestion: Verify that Figures 5, 6, 7 PDFs exist and their captions reflect the null-result framing (Figure 5 caption should note rates are below shuffled control; Figure 6 caption should note coherence is suggestive pending replication). Write Appendix B (D-EDA conditioning analysis) and Appendix C (RAVEL probe accuracy table and shuffled control 9-row table). Resolve the SynthSAEBench citation: if constructed by this work, describe it as 'a synthetic benchmark constructed following standard absorption injection (details in Appendix A)' and remove the external citation.

findings.description: The paper title ('Where and When Encoder-Decoder Misalignment Signals Feature Absorption') promises a crisp answer to 'where and when' but the paper does not present this answer in a user-accessible format. The operating regime (mid-layer, narrow SAEs) is described in the Discussion but no summary table or decision rule is provided in the paper's main flow.
findings.suggestion: Add a single-sentence 'operating regime summary' in Section 7.1 or the Conclusion: 'EDA is reliable for absorption detection at SAE widths <= 16k and transformer layers in the early-to-mid range (approximately the first 60% of layers); it fails at widths >= 65k and at deeper layers.' This directly answers the title's promise.

findings.description: Rho notation inconsistency: the paper uses $\rho$ without subscript for Spearman correlations throughout (abstract, Section 5.3, Section 7.2), but the notation.md specifies $\rho_s$ for Spearman. Also, the abstract ITAC result reads '3.14% mean false-negative reduction' when the actual FN rate change is 7.6% -> 6.2%, a reduction of 1.4 percentage points (not 3.14 percentage points). The 3.14% is a relative reduction figure; the absolute change is 1.4pp. This is ambiguous without qualification.
findings.suggestion: Use $\rho_s$ for Spearman throughout (per notation.md). Clarify the ITAC result: '3.14% relative FN reduction (7.6% to 6.2% absolute parent FN rate, a 1.4 percentage point reduction)' to avoid ambiguity about whether the 3.14% is absolute or relative.

metric_gaming_risks: The 'early absorption dominance' finding (72-75%) is highly threshold-sensitive: at tau=0.2 the split is 32%/34%/34%, at tau=0.3 it is 72%/14%/14%, at tau=0.4 it is 95%/3%/2%. The paper's central actionable claim is contingent on an unjustified threshold choice.

EDA AUROC at L12-16k (0.776) uses Neuronpedia proxy labels with n_pos=16 absorbed latents, identical to the L12-65k pilot setup that collapsed from AUROC=0.853 to 0.468 in the full run. The L12-16k full result may be equally inflated; there is no direct-label validation for any Gemma Scope configuration.
Bootstrap CIs with n_pos=3 (polysemantic AUROC stratum) are numerically unreliable and present a false impression of precision for the 0.922 [0.842, 0.979] figure.

The intra-RAVEL coherence (rho=0.924) reflects correlated measurement artifacts from the same invalid probe methodology applied to the same SAEs — not necessarily genuine absorption coherence. The R4 pilot with n=3 configs gives rho=0.667, suggesting the R3 rho=0.924 is sensitive to configuration selection.
ITAC's 3.14% 'mean FN reduction' is dominated by zeros (9/10 targets show 0% or negative reduction) and one outlier (j_idx=61217: 18.9%). The mean is not representative of the typical outcome.
novelty_risks: EDA = 1 - encoder_decoder_cosine_sim is already in SAEBench. The novelty claim reduces to the formal theorem (which is in proof-sketch form) and the empirical AUROC evaluation (which shows regime-specific results). A reviewer may assess this as 'empirical evaluation of an existing metric with an incomplete theorem.'

The three-subtype taxonomy (early/late/partial) formalizes a natural two-way distinction (feature not learned vs. feature learned but suppressed) by adding a 'partial' category whose EDA behavior contradicts the predicted ordering. The taxonomy may not be perceived as a distinct contribution by reviewers who see the early/late dichotomy as obvious.

The cross-domain contribution has been downgraded to a null result with a conditional coherence observation. This leaves the paper with two primary contributions (EDA detection + taxonomy), both of which have limitations identified above.

The early-absorption dominance finding — the most potentially impactful result — rests on n=16 and n=65 latents in two configurations with a threshold-sensitive classification. This is insufficient to 'reframe the field' as implied by the proposal.
reproducibility_gaps: Gemma 2B and Llama-3.1-8B are HF-gated; all Gemma Scope AUROC values use proxy labels and cannot be independently reproduced without HuggingFace access to those models.

The RAVEL cross-domain analysis requires Qwen2.5-0.5B probes trained on RAVEL data and projected via QR decomposition to Gemma's d_model=2304; this preprocessing is not standard and requires specific code from this work.

ITAC is evaluated on synthetic activations (decoder columns as parent-positive inputs), not real model activations. A practitioner attempting to reproduce or apply ITAC will obtain different results on real text.
Appendix B (D-EDA conditioning analysis) and Appendix C (RAVEL probe accuracy table) are cited in the main text but absent from paper.md.

The SynthSAEBench citation ('synthasbench2026') requires verification; if this is an internally constructed benchmark, an external citation is invalid and the benchmark is unreproducible without the generation code.
Figure 5 and Figure 6 captions need updating to reflect null-result framing; submitting with outdated captions creates inconsistency between text and visuals.
